interactions.summary: Jiang frames the class as speculative tool-use rather than truth, then argues that modern Gaza should be interpreted through an older pattern of public ritual sacrifice rather than only ordinary military cruelty.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture; censored written words are not transcribed.

interactions.summary: A student asks what the river is in Jiang's Chinese military analogy; Jiang answers that the taboo itself is the river, because killing children creates no exit except escalation or destruction.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Mostly Jiang, with a brief student question embedded in the lecture segment.

interactions.summary: Jiang pivots from the disturbing political example into a thought experiment about an island, a safe hill, and flesh-eating monkeys as a model for how impossible danger creates group cohesion.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: Jiang builds the Monkey Island thought experiment: desperate strangers trapped among flesh-eating enemies generate language, myth, ritual, leadership, and transformation through danger.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: He defines synchronicity through sports teams, maternal intuition, and soldiers jumping on grenades: the group becomes a hive mind in which self-preservation yields to comrades.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture with rhetorical questions to students.

interactions.summary: Jiang extends Monkey Island into a secret-elite origin story: the men return to ordinary society with memories, rituals, religion, and a hive mind that later lets their descendants control power.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: A student asks how returned peasants could matter if society already has leaders; Jiang answers that surface leaders are not real power and promises historical analogies.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question in seg-0019; Jiang answer in seg-0020.

interactions.summary: He turns to Sparta: Thermopylae, Leonidas, harsh childhood hazing, same-sex mentorship, and helot terror become historical analogues for sacrifice-generated cohesion.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: Jiang extends the Spartan analogy through helot killing, the Sacred Band of Thebes, and Macedonia, arguing that sacrificial lover-bands create armies that welcome death and become dominant.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: He introduces game theory: winning requires cheating and secret coordination, and transgression is presented as the hidden coordination method that avoids triggering everyone else's arms race.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture with rhetorical prompts.

interactions.summary: Jiang escalates transgression from pranks and theft to ultimate taboos, arguing that taboo-breaking feels empowering, addictive, and in some secret-society frames releases divine energy.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture, with repeated disclaimers that he is theorizing and condemning the acts.

interactions.summary: He begins the deep theory: Kant says humans do not passively absorb reality but filter noumena into phenomena; Hegel's Geist is introduced as the spiritual solution to Kant's problems.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: Jiang defines Geist as the real invisible spiritual world, then moves through Plato and Gnosticism to the Monad, dyads, layered realities, and the purpose of returning to the source.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: He contrasts Plato's knowledge path with Dante's love path, then uses free will, the material prison, science, and transgression as parts of a speculative model for why evil persists and coordinates.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture.

interactions.summary: A student asks whether governments help or are controlled by elderly pensioners; Jiang answers that in Chinese society respect for elders makes elderly cultural and political priority structurally unsurprising.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question in seg-0049; Jiang answer in seg-0050.

interactions.summary: Amber asks Jiang to restate what evil is in the Monad model; Jiang defines evil as denial of the system, distance from Monad/love/knowledge, and excessive embrace of the material world, then Amber asks whether sacrifice can still create cohesion.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Amber/student in seg-0051, seg-0053, seg-0056; Jiang in seg-0052 and seg-0054.

interactions.summary: Jiang answers Amber's sacrifice question: sacrifice can unlock divine energy because it is performed inside a religious framework and accesses lower evil forces rather than the Monad.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang answer with brief student acknowledgments.

interactions.summary: Amber tries to reconcile Dante's love model with evil deeds and the socially constructed Monad; Jiang briefly confirms one step before the question continues into the next packet.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student in seg-0062 and seg-0064; Jiang in seg-0063.

interactions.summary: Jiang answers Amber by saying current society is anti-love: school makes parents deny children's free will and care about grades and elite college admission more than happiness; Amber restates that denial of free will is anti-love and evil.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang in seg-0065 and seg-0066; Amber/student in seg-0067; Jiang confirms in seg-0068.

interactions.summary: Another student asks why power prevents people from approaching the Monad; Jiang answers that power rationalizes itself through rival interpretations, material-world systems, and ideas that preserve control even when they deny truth.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student in seg-0069 and seg-0071; Jiang in seg-0070 and seg-0072.

interactions.summary: Jiang pauses to restate that the model is only a theory and that he does not know whether it is true.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang.

interactions.summary: Students try to clarify whether the Monad has different meanings; Jiang insists it has one meaning with many names, while Plato and Dante debate different paths of return: knowledge and love.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student questions in SPEAKER_00 and SPEAKER_04; Jiang in SPEAKER_01.

interactions.summary: A student asks again whether Plato and Dante give ways to approach the Monad; Jiang confirms and sharpens the contrast between Plato's elite geometry/knowledge path and Dante's universal love path.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student in SPEAKER_00; Jiang in SPEAKER_01.

interactions.summary: Another student asks why different religions converge on similar ultimate reality; Jiang answers that ideas come from and return to the Geist, which explains cross-cultural inspiration better than neuroscience alone.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student in SPEAKER_04; Jiang in SPEAKER_01.

speaker_notes.note: Diarized SPEAKER_01 is Jiang across the packet; one student question is folded into seg-0007 rather than separately diarized.
Several phrases are ASR-damaged in the Gaza/religion passage; preserve uncertainty around specific named terms while extracting only the clear argumentative structure.
Continuous Jiang monologue; student-facing questions are rhetorical and answered by Jiang.
SPEAKER_00 is a student asking about whether already-existing leaders would block returned Monkey Island men from power.
Continuous Jiang monologue; diarization stays stable.
Continuous Jiang monologue; the class-break sound in seg-0033 is acknowledged but does not change the interaction.
Continuous Jiang monologue; 'Danae/Danteng' in the transcript refers to Dante.
This packet is mostly student Q&A; SPEAKER_02, SPEAKER_03, and possibly SPEAKER_00 are students, while SPEAKER_01 is Jiang.
seg-0055 is an empty residue after boundary repair and should not carry semantic content.
Short student acknowledgments and follow-ups are interleaved with Jiang's answer; SPEAKER_03 is Amber/student.
Q&A packet; SPEAKER_03 appears to be Amber, SPEAKER_00 another student, and SPEAKER_01 Jiang.
SPEAKER_00 asks repeated clarification questions; SPEAKER_04 briefly rephrases; SPEAKER_01 is Jiang managing the Q&A.
Final Q&A; SPEAKER_00 and SPEAKER_04 are students, SPEAKER_01 is Jiang.

claims.claim: Jiang explicitly presents the lecture as speculative theorizing: the ideas are tools for understanding the world, not gospel or final truth.
He argues that public mass killing has a recurring sacrificial form, citing Aztec, Phoenician/Carthaginian, and Roman examples before extending the pattern to Gaza.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical analogy advanced in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: His Gaza argument depends on public visibility: he says the act is done in front of the world not despite global hatred but because hatred helps generate the taboo that binds the group.
claims.temporal_scope: Contemporary political diagnosis in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: The river-at-the-back analogy models taboo as a forced no-exit condition: once the worst modern taboo is crossed, the group either escalates together or is destroyed.
In Jiang's island model, existential danger produces a new common language, founding myth, religion, rituals, and leader among otherwise unrelated men.
He argues that sacrificial commitment, not mere cleverness, selects the leader in a crisis group: the one who shows willingness to lose a hand wins devotion over the wise adviser.
Cohesion in the model becomes synchronicity: people from different backgrounds learn to understand each other more intimately than their own families and act as one mind.
Jiang uses the grenade example to argue that under extreme synchronicity the body acts before calculation, sacrificing itself for the group because the team matters more than individual life.
Jiang claims that the Monkey Island group's intense shared ordeal would survive return to normal life as a secret religion, ritual inheritance, and elite power network.
He distinguishes visible leadership from real power: presidents and famous figures may appear to rule, while the cohesive inherited network controls things underneath.
Jiang uses Spartan education as evidence that ritualized brutality, hazing, mentorship, and sexual bonds can manufacture brotherhood and military synchronicity.
Jiang says Sparta's killing of helots functioned as sacrifice: it made Sparta hated, but also unified and militarily dominant.
He uses the Sacred Band of Thebes and Macedonia as a chain of copied military-cohesion systems, culminating in soldiers who stand in place to die so others can escape.
Jiang's game-theory model says rule-followers cannot win; to win, players cheat by coordinating with others, but must do so secretly to avoid provoking rival coordination.

Transgression is defined as breaking taboos, social norms, and social laws; Jiang proposes that greater transgression creates greater cohesion and synchronicity because secrecy becomes necessary for survival.
claims.temporal_scope: Definition and model in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that transgression feels empowering and addictive because breaking the rule makes the actor feel more powerful than parents, teachers, or authority.
He proposes that extreme secret-society rituals combine group unity, synchronicity, and a belief that divine power has been accessed through taboo violation.
Kant's model is summarized as active filtering: humans cannot know the noumenal thing-in-itself, only the phenomenal world produced by mental filters such as space and time.
claims.temporal_scope: Philosophical model introduced in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Hegel's Geist is introduced as the answer to Kant's three problems: what reality is, who gives humans filters, and how people perceive the same world.
claims.temporal_scope: Philosophical model introduced in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says Geist answers Kant's problems by making the real world spiritual rather than material, giving humans filters, and aligning human perceptions through a shared source.
The Monad is introduced as the one, the spiritual sun, or supreme source whose emanations create dyads and layered realities, with earth as the outer shell.
Jiang contrasts two routes back to the Monad: Plato's pursuit of knowledge and Dante's pursuit of love.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative philosophy model in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Dante's answer to evil is free will: the Monad loves humans, refuses to override their agency, and sends messengers whose ideas people may accept or reject.
claims.temporal_scope: Dante model as reconstructed in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: The powerful maintain power by making the material world feel real, denying the spiritual model, and using transgression to tune into the same lower spiritual 'website' for synchronicity.
claims.temporal_scope: Speculative power model in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says elderly pensioners control Chinese society culturally and politically because respect for elders is built into Chinese social structure from childhood.
claims.temporal_scope: Answer during the 2025-08-29 lecture, referring back to prior class discussion.

claims.claim: Within the Monad system, evil is distance from the Monad: denying truth, love, knowledge, or the spiritual world and embracing the material shadow world.
Jiang contrasts Plato and Dante again: for Plato, good is pursuit of knowledge and evil includes denial of knowledge and shadow-making art; for Dante, more love is good and less love is bad.
Jiang says sacrifice can unlock divine energy because it is not isolated violence but a ritual consummation of a religion that worships and sends victims to a god.
In this metaphysical model, sacrificial religion accesses a lower part of the universe populated by evil beings rather than returning upward toward the Monad.
Amber's question identifies a tension in Dante's theory: if evil deeds create love or cohesion, she asks whether that could still bring people closer to the Monad.

Jiang says contemporary society is anti-love because institutions such as school distort parent-child relationships from happiness and agency toward grades, elite college admission, and future employment anxiety.
Amber accurately restates Jiang's model: denying free will is anti-love, therefore evil, and limits movement toward the Monad.
claims.temporal_scope: Student restatement confirmed in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says the powerful maintain power by developing a religion and systems that justify material-world control, even if those systems invert the Monad as evil and deny truth.
Jiang repeats that the entire framework is speculative and not known to be true.
The Monad has one meaning despite many names: the divine sun, source of all life, one and all, infinite, eternal, perfect, and immutable.
Plato and Dante are not defining different Monads; they are debating how humans return to the same source, through knowledge or love.
Jiang says multiple religious traditions, including Buddhism and Hinduism, share the same underlying conception of ultimate reality despite local differences.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative religion claim in the 2025-08-29 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang says Plato's route to the Monad is elitist because it requires mathematical and geometric knowledge that only a few can access.
Dante's route is universal because if the Monad loves everyone, everyone must have an accessible capacity to return to it, and that capacity is love.
Jiang proposes Geist as the answer to why distant religions converge: ideas are inspired through communication with the Geist, rather than simply produced by brain synapses.
He says neuroscience avoids the basic question of where thoughts come from because it cannot answer it within a purely biological frame.

signature_moments.moment: The lecture begins by disarming truth-claims: these are not beliefs to obey, but tools to test against the world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: These are tools.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: That methodological warning frames the whole lecture's extreme claims as a model to evaluate rather than doctrine.

signature_moments.moment: The Roman triumph is re-read as disguised sacrifice: victory parade becomes ritual execution at Jupiter's temple.
signature_moments.source_phrase: That's sacrifice. That's human sacrifice.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It supplies the historical grammar Jiang uses to reinterpret modern public violence.

signature_moments.moment: A taboo becomes a river behind the army: the horror blocks retreat and creates unity.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the river is a taboo
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the lecture its first compact causal model for how evil can produce solidarity.

signature_moments.moment: Monkey Island is hell as a social engine: no escape, infinite enemies, and strangers forced to invent a people.
signature_moments.source_phrase: there's no escape from this island
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The image lets Jiang model nation, myth, brotherhood, and elite cohesion as products of impossible pressure.

signature_moments.moment: The speech loses to the severed hand: wisdom advises, but visible sacrifice rules.
signature_moments.source_phrase: he cuts off his hand in front of everyone
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It compresses Jiang's leadership theory into a brutal selection mechanism.

signature_moments.moment: Synchronicity is a hive mind that makes the soldier jump on the grenade before thinking.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you just do it
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The moment makes cohesion physical: coordination becomes reflex, not agreement.

signature_moments.moment: The men come home from hell and quietly inherit the world.
signature_moments.source_phrase: secretly you're the ones who are in control
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's bridge from thought experiment to conspiracy-like power model.

signature_moments.moment: Spartan childhood becomes a machine for turning terror into love between boys.
signature_moments.source_phrase: only by helping each other can they survive the brutality
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives historical flesh to Jiang's claim that suffering can manufacture cohesion.

signature_moments.moment: The Sacred Band blocks the Macedonian charge not because survival matters, but because death is honor.
signature_moments.source_phrase: They did not fear death. They welcomed death.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It condenses the sacrifice-to-power mechanism in a historical scene.

signature_moments.moment: Game theory turns morality upside down: the best way to win is to cheat, but secretly.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you follow the rules you will never win the game
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reversal connects taboo-breaking to strategic advantage rather than random wickedness.

signature_moments.moment: A prank begins the same mechanism as darker acts: secret rule-breaking makes a group feel more powerful than authority.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you broke the rules
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The ordinary prank makes the lecture's taboo theory scalable from school mischief to ritual violence.

signature_moments.moment: The ultimate taboo is imagined as an energy source: the forbidden act floods the violator with a sense of cosmic power.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they found the ultimate power in the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the hinge from social secrecy into the lecture's metaphysics of evil.

signature_moments.moment: Reality is not absorbed; the mind makes the world by filtering what it can never directly know.
signature_moments.source_phrase: our brains are filters
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Kant becomes the philosophical license for asking where ideas and shared worlds come from.

signature_moments.moment: Geist is unpacked through ghost, geyser, and gist: parallel world, erupting force, and essence.
signature_moments.source_phrase: ghost geyser and just
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The folk-etymology gives Jiang's abstract Hegel passage a memorable three-part image.

signature_moments.moment: Earth is only the outer shell of a multi-layered Geist.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the most outer shell in this universe
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reverses ordinary materialism and lets the later argument treat power as spiritual alignment.

signature_moments.moment: Dante makes love the divine spark that lets anyone return to the source.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this divine spark is called love
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the lecture its positive counter-model to transgression: love as universal access.

signature_moments.moment: Evil coordination is imagined like everyone secretly logging into the same website.
signature_moments.source_phrase: locked on to the same website
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The internet metaphor makes the metaphysical claim operational: shared transgression becomes shared frequency.

signature_moments.moment: Evil is not a monster first; it is movement away from the source.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the further you move away from the Monad, that's what's evil
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives the read its cleanest definition of evil inside Jiang's metaphysical frame.

signature_moments.moment: Movies are shadows of a shadow world, making Plato's prison even thicker.
signature_moments.source_phrase: movies are just a shadow of the shadow world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is a sharp Jiang-style aside connecting art, illusion, and Plato's suspicion of representation.

signature_moments.moment: Sacrifice is not done for sacrifice's sake; it is the consummation of a religion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a consummation of their religion
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This line prevents the episode from treating sacrifice as simple cruelty; Jiang is describing an internal ritual logic.

signature_moments.moment: The ritual goes lower, not higher.
signature_moments.source_phrase: they go lower
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives Jiang's metaphysics of evil a vertical geography: love ascends, transgression descends.

signature_moments.moment: School turns parental love into anxiety over grades.
signature_moments.source_phrase: because of school, parents care about your grades
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It grounds the metaphysical model in an ordinary institution students immediately know.

signature_moments.moment: Power does not merely disobey the Monad; it rewrites the map so its own god becomes good.
signature_moments.source_phrase: In their conception, the Monad's the evil one. Their god's the good one.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the strongest explanation of why evil can feel righteous from inside the system.

signature_moments.moment: The most extreme model is interrupted by an epistemic brake: no one knows if this is true.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I don't know, okay?
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is essential public framing for a controversial lecture: Jiang is presenting a theory, not a settled doctrine.

signature_moments.moment: The Monad is the sun, the one and all, the infinite and eternal.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the divine sun, the source of all life
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode a compact image for an otherwise abstract metaphysical source.

signature_moments.moment: Dante rejects a heaven only reachable by people who can do sacred geometry.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The Monad would not create a system which only the elitist can access.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes Dante's love path feel like a democratic revolt against philosophical elitism.

signature_moments.moment: Ideas come from the Geist and go back into it; the mind is a receiver, not just a brain firing synapses.
signature_moments.source_phrase: our ideas come from the Geist
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This closes the lecture by connecting religious convergence, imagination, and Jiang's critique of materialism.

signature_moments.moment: Geist is a ghost: with us and not with us.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It is with us and it is not with us.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The closing image gives an abstract Hegelian concept a memorable felt texture.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's category for public killing that is not merely instrumental but creates religious, social, or political cohesion through taboo and display.
A Chinese military strategy used as an analogy for forcing unity by removing the possibility of retreat.
The forbidden act whose violation creates hatred from outside and no-exit solidarity inside the violating group.
Jiang's thought experiment for how hopeless shared danger turns strangers into a mythic, ritualized, highly cohesive group.
People acting in unison because their minds have become alike; Jiang also calls it a hive mind and links it to telepathy-like intuition.
A state of group perception in which members sense and act for one another without explicit communication.
The hidden power network Jiang imagines arising from inherited shared ordeal, ritual, and synchronicity.
Ritualized brutality that Jiang says builds solidarity because victims survive by helping one another.
The enslaved conquered people in Sparta whom Jiang says young Spartans were sent to kill as a graduation ritual.
Breaking taboos, social norms, or laws in a way that creates secret mutual dependence and therefore cohesion.
The Theban lover-soldier unit Jiang uses as an example of voluntary bonded military cohesion.
Jiang's game-theory problem in which visible collaboration forces rivals to collaborate too, making secrecy essential.
The power secret transgressors believe they access by committing forbidden acts; Jiang presents this as their superstition inside his speculative model.
Kant's objective reality or thing-in-itself, which humans cannot directly know in Jiang's summary.
The world humans experience after mental filters interact with noumena.
Hegel's spirit-world concept, presented by Jiang as the deeper reality that creates the material world and human filters.
The one, spiritual sun, or supreme source of reality to which Plato and Dante offer different return paths.
Pairs of forces or beings created by emanation from the Monad that generate different realities in Jiang's summary.
The Monad's greatest gift in Jiang's Dante model; it explains why evil can happen without divine interference.
In the Monad model, denial of the spiritual system and movement away from truth, knowledge, and love.
The prior-class model the student references, where elderly pensioners control society because culture and government orient around them.
Jiang's phrase for sacrifice as the ritual completion of a religious demand, not violence detached from belief.
The region of Jiang's metaphysical map associated with evil beings and sacrificial transgression.
A society structured so normal human relationships cannot flourish because institutions deny free will and agency.
The concrete form of anti-love in Jiang's answer: replacing a person's agency with institutional demands.
Jiang's image for the Monad as the single source of all life.
The spiritual aim shared by Plato and Dante in Jiang's model, though they propose different paths.
Jiang's shorthand for Plato's demanding knowledge path toward the Monad.
The model that ideas are received from and returned to Geist, explaining cross-cultural religious convergence.

chronology_notes.note: The Gaza diagnosis is a dated position from 2025-08-29 and should not be generalized as Jiang's undated or latest position without later source comparison.
Jiang says Kant, Plato, Hegel, and Dante will be developed more deeply later in the semester; this lecture is an introductory framework dated 2025-08-29.
The pensioner answer explicitly refers back to the prior class and should be compared with Secret History #3 in any corpus-impact pass.
The school/parent claim may connect to earlier schooling and anti-agency materials, but this packet itself only supplies the dated 2025-08-29 formulation.
The lecture closes by saying the class will continue this next time, so later Secret History lectures may refine this Geist/evil framework.

uncertainty_notes.note: The religious-tradition passage contains severe ASR damage; extract the clear claim that sacrifice is said to unite the Israeli population, but do not rely on garbled named terms.
Claims about Gaza are Jiang's controversial interpretation in this lecture, not independently verified fact.
Some ritual-language detail is ASR-damaged; the clear semantic movement is language, myth, ritual, sex, and leadership as cohesion devices.
The funeral ritual sentence is ASR-damaged; preserve only that Jiang says strange rituals around sacrifice help maintain unity.
Several Greek names and battle names are ASR-damaged; semantic extraction keeps Jiang's intended Thebes/Macedon/Sacred Band chain without correcting public transcript text.
One sentence inside the transgression definition contains multilingual ASR noise; the surrounding definition remains clear.
Secret-society claims are explicitly framed by Jiang as possible theory, not asserted proof.
The transcript renders 'gist' as 'just'; extraction follows Jiang's apparent ghost/geyser/gist explanation.
Some emanation vocabulary is ASR-damaged; semantic extraction keeps the clear Monad/dyad/layered-reality structure.
The student names the board model; the transcript briefly interrupts with Jiang's 'The Monad.'
Amber's question is cut off and continues into the next packet; do not treat it as a complete claim by Jiang.
One middle phrase in the school example is ASR-damaged, but the institutional anti-love claim is clear.
The transcript renders Monad as 'Nomad' in several student questions; semantic extraction treats the intended term as Monad.
The transcript alternates Monad/Nomad and Dante/Danteng; semantic extraction uses the intended terms.
